sources_exist.note: Opened all 9 evidence_facts[].url occurrences live (8 unique URLs); every occurrence resolved, including the PDF and both occurrences of the Clio Legal Trends URL.

figures_match_sources.note: All 9 exact contiguous quotes were located, but only 6 atomic facts passed quote/title/attribution and every scope field. F3, F8, and F9 fail strict geography and/or population scope verification.

judgment_inputs_plausible.note: The exact ESTIMATE_HEAVY flag is confirmed at 12 estimate-provenance inputs, and LOW_CONFIDENCE is appropriate, but the ai_replaceable_share, historical_analogs, and owners_60plus_pct estimates have incomplete atomic-fact or population/search bridges.

rubric_consistent.note: The run schema and v3.1.1 semantic checks pass; naics/run/template/finalizer identities match; all four deterministic dataset objects and every role SOC/title/wage share match; the residual, provenance mapping, contributions, factors, S, and t50 boundary recompute exactly. S=2.9507430054231993 confirms the exact BORDERLINE: |S - pass cut 3.00| = 0.0493 flag, and all three assigned Stage-4 flags recompute exactly.

cross_checks_honest.note: The uplift and pricing-deflation narratives are broadly consistent, but no_proxy_confirmed contradicts the actual F4/F5 use and obscures repeated use of the ownership mechanism, while terminal_value substitutes ownership restrictions for the required paid-demand human boundary analysis.

source_audits.note: Resolved. The exact quote, page heading, May 8, 2025 date, solo/small population, unit, and disclosed target-population mismatch are supported; the page does not identify the statistic's respondent geography.

Resolved. The exact 19%-to-93% quote, page heading, March 25, 2025 date, and mid-sized-firm context are present; the release does not define the firm-size population or give a study geography, as the mismatch discloses.

Resolved. The PDF title, exact 90% quote, Legal Tracker attribution, and 2025 LDO Index footnote are present, but scope.geography says 'not reported' even though the containing report explicitly identifies its geography as the US legal market.

Resolved. The Rule 5.4 title and exact fee-sharing sentence appear contiguously, and the population, model-rule unit, unreported jurisdictional geography, current-page period, and model-rule mismatch are candid.

Resolved. The Arizona Supreme Court page contains the exact ABS definition and Rules 31/31.1(c) attribution; Arizona population/geography, regulatory-definition unit, current-page period, and state-specific mismatch are supported.

Resolved. The Demographics page contains the exact over-13% age-65-plus sentence and the adjacent 2023 CPS table; US-lawyer population, share unit, period, and owner-population mismatch are supported.

Resolved. The exact Legal Services and Law Firms row appears under the Full-Year 2025 sector table, and its headers support the 13 sales, prices, revenue/cash-flow figures and multiples, and days-on-market scope; sector-table geography is not stated.

Resolved. The 59% quote, 2024 period, report title, population, and unit match, but scope.geography incorrectly says 'not reported'; the report methodology states the analyzed Clio customers were located in the United States.

Resolved as this separate URL occurrence. The exact 45% quote and report title match, but the source identifies a survey of 1,702 U.S. legal professionals; the fact instead labels the population as law firms and geography as 'not reported'.

reasons: evidence_facts[2].scope.geography says 'not reported', but the cited PDF is explicitly the 2026 Report on the State of the US Legal Market. Correct the geography to the disclosed report context, or precisely distinguish and document a different Legal Tracker data geography.

evidence_facts[7].scope.geography says 'not reported', while the cited report's methodology says included Clio customers were located in the United States. Correct the geography and retain the Clio-platform population limitation.

evidence_facts[8].scope.population and scope.geography describe unqualified law firms with geography 'not reported', but the cited result comes from a survey of 1,702 U.S. legal professionals. Correct both scope fields to the respondent population and United States geography.

inputs.ai_replaceable_share.estimate_basis.bridge_steps attributes document-automation and virtual-receptionist experimentation to F1 and a lawyer-judgment boundary to F4, although F1's atomic quote states only adoption percentages and F4's atomic quote states only fee sharing. Add atomic facts for those background claims or rewrite them as explicit uncited judgments with appropriately reassessed evidence quality.

inputs.historical_analogs.value asserts that no quantified historical analogue was found, but its estimate_basis discloses no historical-analogue searches and cites only two current, non-comparable adoption observations. Disclose the searches and results supporting the absence finding, or reframe the selection and evidence quality so it does not imply an audited search.

inputs.owners_60plus_pct.rationale and estimate_basis rely on exact 13.1% and 17.9% age-band figures that are absent from F6's atomic quote, and they never provide the required population-mapping step from all lawyers to law-firm owners. Add an atomic age-table fact and an explicit owner-population bridge, or remove the unsupported quantified claims.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed says ownership-rule evidence is used only for B and M, but F4 is cited by ai_replaceable_share (V), F4 and F5 are cited by pi_moat (C), and the same ownership mechanism also appears in the active-consolidator and terminal narratives. Rewrite the cross-check to match actual use and ensure the ownership mechanism is not counted repeatedly across V, C, B, and terminal logic.

cross_checks.terminal_value.justification uses a nonlawyer-ownership rule as its human boundary but does not analyze whether licensure, required signing or appearance, and malpractice liability preserve paid demand. Complete that independent terminal analysis and separate paid-demand durability from the ownership effects already used in C and B.
